era_context: Egypt's Old Kingdom, Dynasty IV (c. 2613–2494 BCE). The reign of Khufu or his immediate successors; the age of pyramid construction at Giza. Contemporaneously, the Akkad Empire was consolidating power in Mesopotamia under Sargon of Akkad (r. c. 2334–2279 BCE) — though this is slightly later. In the Indus Valley, early urban centres were developing. This is the height of Egyptian monumental architecture and centralised pharaonic power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Mesopotamia (Akkad/Sumeria) — limited specific records for this year
Indus Valley Civilisation — no written records survive; only archaeological evidence
Sub-Saharan Africa — virtually no surviving documentation

events.title: Ongoing construction or completion phases of Giza pyramid complex
events.description: The Giza pyramid complex, including the Great Pyramid of Khufu, was either under active construction or in its final phases during this period. The exact chronology of Khufu's reign remains debated (c. 2589–2566 BCE in conventional dating), but monumental construction, quarrying, and labour organisation at Giza represent the peak of Old Kingdom engineering and administrative capacity. The pyramids served as royal tombs and monuments to pharaonic power and divine kingship.

sources.name: Pyramid Texts and Old Kingdom administrative records (papyri from Wadi el-Jarf)
Archaeological evidence from Giza excavations (Zahi Hawass, Mark Lehner)

events.certainty_note: Khufu's reign is conventionally dated c. 2589–2566 BCE, placing 2549 BCE within or near his reign. However, absolute chronology for the Old Kingdom remains contested; some scholars propose dates up to 100 years earlier or later. The construction of Giza is archaeologically confirmed, but the precise year-by-year timeline is not recoverable.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional dating of Khufu's reign (and thus the Giza pyramids) is not universally accepted. Some Egyptologists argue for earlier dates (c. 2650 BCE) based on radiocarbon analysis and astronomical alignments, while others defend the traditional chronology. The 'Giza construction timeline' is inferred from stratigraphy and administrative records, not from year-specific contemporary documentation. No events for 2549 BCE specifically are attested in surviving sources; this entry represents a best-estimate placement within a broader construction phase.
historiographic_note: The historical record for Egypt in Dynasty IV is dominated by monumental and administrative evidence (temple reliefs, administrative papyri, tomb inscriptions). The surviving sources are heavily biased toward royal and elite perspectives. Lower-class labour, daily life, and non-monumental activities are largely invisible. Absolute chronology for the Old Kingdom depends on radiocarbon dating, king lists (which contain gaps and inconsistencies), and astronomical calculations — all of which have margins of error of ±50–100 years. The year 2549 BCE is assigned by convention within a broader era, not by specific contemporary attestation.

graph_edges.note: Giza construction is part of Khufu's reign-long project
